context:
Section 2.1.1 (Regularized bicausal OT), Remark 2.1, page 7.

This paper focuses on existence and uniqueness of solutions introduced in Definition 2.2. Convergence of numerical algorithms for the transport problem with time-inconsistent costs remains a problem. Notably, even in the static case with quadratic regularization, this problem has only been examined in a recent work by González-Sanz and Nutz (2024).
title: Prove convergence of numerical algorithms for time-inconsistent equilibrium bicausal transport
background_latex:
Consider a finite time horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(\mu\in\mathcal{P}(X_{1:T})\) and \(\nu\in\mathcal{P}(Y_{1:T})\) be Borel probability measures on path spaces \(X_{1:T}=X_1\times\cdots\times X_T\) and \(Y_{1:T}=Y_1\times\cdots\times Y_T\) (each \(X_t,Y_t\) Polish). A (bicausal) transport plan \(\pi\) is a coupling of \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) whose successive conditional kernels satisfy the non-anticipativity constraints in both directions; denote the set of such plans by \(\Pi_{\mathrm{bc}}(\mu,\nu)\).

A central feature is a time-inconsistent objective functional, where the criterion evaluated at time \(t\) depends on the conditional law of the future under \(\pi\) in a way that breaks the tower property/dynamic programming. One important class discussed is bicausal optimal transport with non-separable \(f\)-divergence regularization. In the discrete (finite-support) case, for a convex function \(f\) and a pathwise cost \(c(x_{1:T},y_{1:T})\), the regularized objective at time 0 takes the form
\[
\inf_{\pi\in\Pi_{\mathrm{bc}}(\mu,\nu)}\ \langle c,\pi\rangle + \big\langle f\big(\tfrac{\pi}{\mu\otimes\nu}\big),\mu\otimes\nu\big\rangle,
\]
where \(\mu\otimes\nu\) is the independent coupling and the inner products denote finite sums over paths. For non-separable choices of \(f\) (e.g., squared Hellinger distance, Le Cam distance, Jensen–Shannon divergence), the resulting dynamic problem is time-inconsistent.

To address time inconsistency, the paper defines an equilibrium (subgame-perfect) bicausal transport plan \(\pi^*\) via one-step deviations: at each time \(t\), conditioning on the realized past \((x_{1:t},y_{1:t})\), the conditional kernel for \((x_{t+1},y_{t+1})\) minimizes the current objective given that later kernels are fixed to those of \(\pi^*\). This yields an extended dynamic programming recursion that characterizes \(\pi^*\) and its value functions. Practical computation of such equilibria typically requires numerical algorithms (e.g., dynamic-programming-based solvers and regularized OT-type methods) on discretized state spaces and/or via parametric representations.
problem_definition_latex: Design and analyze numerical algorithms for computing an equilibrium bicausal transport plan \(\pi^*\) for time-inconsistent transport objectives (in particular, non-separable \(f\)-divergence regularized bicausal optimal transport), and prove convergence of these algorithms to an equilibrium transport (or to the associated equilibrium value function) under mathematically stated conditions.
latex:
Consider a finite time horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(\mu\in\mathcal{P}(X_{1:T})\) and \(\nu\in\mathcal{P}(Y_{1:T})\) be Borel probability measures on path spaces \(X_{1:T}=X_1\times\cdots\times X_T\) and \(Y_{1:T}=Y_1\times\cdots\times Y_T\) (each \(X_t,Y_t\) Polish). A (bicausal) transport plan \(\pi\) is a coupling of \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) whose successive conditional kernels satisfy the non-anticipativity constraints in both directions; denote the set of such plans by \(\Pi_{\mathrm{bc}}(\mu,\nu)\).

A central feature is a time-inconsistent objective functional, where the criterion evaluated at time \(t\) depends on the conditional law of the future under \(\pi\) in a way that breaks the tower property/dynamic programming. One important class discussed is bicausal optimal transport with non-separable \(f\)-divergence regularization. In the discrete (finite-support) case, for a convex function \(f\) and a pathwise cost \(c(x_{1:T},y_{1:T})\), the regularized objective at time 0 takes the form
\[
\inf_{\pi\in\Pi_{\mathrm{bc}}(\mu,\nu)}\ \langle c,\pi\rangle + \big\langle f\big(\tfrac{\pi}{\mu\otimes\nu}\big),\mu\otimes\nu\big\rangle,
\]
where \(\mu\otimes\nu\) is the independent coupling and the inner products denote finite sums over paths. For non-separable choices of \(f\) (e.g., squared Hellinger distance, Le Cam distance, Jensen–Shannon divergence), the resulting dynamic problem is time-inconsistent.

To address time inconsistency, the paper defines an equilibrium (subgame-perfect) bicausal transport plan \(\pi^*\) via one-step deviations: at each time \(t\), conditioning on the realized past \((x_{1:t},y_{1:t})\), the conditional kernel for \((x_{t+1},y_{t+1})\) minimizes the current objective given that later kernels are fixed to those of \(\pi^*\). This yields an extended dynamic programming recursion that characterizes \(\pi^*\) and its value functions. Practical computation of such equilibria typically requires numerical algorithms (e.g., dynamic-programming-based solvers and regularized OT-type methods) on discretized state spaces and/or via parametric representations.

Design and analyze numerical algorithms for computing an equilibrium bicausal transport plan \(\pi^*\) for time-inconsistent transport objectives (in particular, non-separable \(f\)-divergence regularized bicausal optimal transport), and prove convergence of these algorithms to an equilibrium transport (or to the associated equilibrium value function) under mathematically stated conditions.
open_problem_quote: This paper focuses on existence and uniqueness of solutions introduced in Definition 2.2. Convergence of numerical algorithms for the transport problem with time-inconsistent costs remains a problem. Notably, even in the static case with quadratic regularization, this problem has only been examined in a recent work by Gonz\'alez-Sanz and Nutz (2024).
subject_classification: computational optimal transport and dynamic equilibrium computation
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question is about proving convergence of algorithms in broad generality rather than refuting a crisp inequality or conjectured bound. There is no clearly specified finite search space of instances or a single theorem statement to falsify via automated counterexample generation.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can implement candidate algorithms and empirically test convergence on many simulated discrete instances, yielding an automatic fitness signal (e.g., residuals of the extended DP optimality conditions). However, the target is a proof of convergence, and the paper does not specify a single canonical algorithm or benchmark theorem to verify automatically, limiting suitability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
